channel.title: Wonders & Oddities General Store
channel.description: Bottled mornings, portable holes, and the occasional Tuesday — test content for theme QA.

wp:category.wp:category_description: Items and articles of general wonder.
The study and safe operation of Portable Holes.
Dispatches from the Left Sock of Destiny department.
Remedies, repellents, and the emotional weather report.
Stories from the shop floor and the back room.
Instructions for handling our more challenging inventory.

item.title: Home
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
Wonders & Oddities General Store stocks products you didn't know you needed and definitely can't explain to your accountant. Bottled mornings, portable holes, moon dust of plausible origin, and the occasional spare Tuesday.

We've been open for approximately one (1) year, depending on which calendar you consult. In that time we've shipped honey to seven continents, including two that aren't recognized by the UN, and received exactly one return — which was, fittingly, a Left Sock of Destiny that the customer reported had started giving unsolicited life advice.

Featured this month

Our Monday Survival Kit bundle is back in stock. Our Mildly Haunted Candle has been relit. And Carl has produced a new batch of Moon Dust, which we have once again not asked him about.

What people are saying

"I bought a Portable Hole (Small) to hide my feelings in. It works exactly as advertised."

— Verified Customer

Browse the shop, wander the Journal, or read about our origins. Stay as long as you like. We have candles.
item.excerpt:encoded: Welcome to Wonders & Oddities. Please wipe your feet on the way in; we have a hole nearby.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Welcome to Wonders & Oddities. Please wipe your feet on the way in; we have a hole nearby.

item.title: Our Story
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
Wonders & Oddities General Store was founded by Percival Aftermath after a personal incident involving a borrowed umbrella, a misunderstood Tuesday, and an unexpected windfall of lunar material from a man named Carl.

The shop's mission, insofar as we have one, is to stock the kind of small, inexplicable objects that make a life feel slightly more plausible. If you have ever wanted to hold silence in your hands, deploy thunder from a pocket, or wear a sock with strong opinions, we are your people.

Our team

The shop currently runs on three human souls and one legally-ambiguous bee supplier. Meet us on the contact page, or read our collected writings in the Journal.

Our suppliers

We decline to name most of our suppliers, partly out of respect and partly to limit our legal exposure. Carl, who supplies the Moon Dust, is an exception. Carl wants to be named. Carl is considering starting his own blog.

Our commitments

  • All products are real, for a given value of "real."
  • No product has ever been tested on animals. Several have been tested on Carl.
  • We ship most orders within 3 business days, except during Spare Tuesday season, which is, by definition, indeterminate.
item.excerpt:encoded: How the shop came to be, and why none of us are allowed to touch the gravity inventory.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: How the shop came to be, and why none of us are allowed to touch the gravity inventory.

item.title: The Shop Journal
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded: The Shop Journal collects writings from our team — Brenda on the handmade pieces, Carl on whatever Carl feels like, Percival on the deeper questions nobody asked him to address. New posts roughly weekly. Older posts appear at the bottom. If you're looking for a specific product story, try the Shop and click through.
item.excerpt:encoded: Dispatches, interviews, how-tos, and the occasional admission of fault.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Dispatches, interviews, how-tos, and the occasional admission of fault.

item.title: Lookbook
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded: Our products are, by design, difficult to photograph. Fog resists the camera. Silence is inherently invisible. Portable Holes ruin light meters. What follows is our best effort.
item.excerpt:encoded: Product photography in the loosest sense of the word.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Product photography in the loosest sense of the word.

item.title: Shipping and Returns
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
Shipping

We ship domestically within 3 business days, internationally within 7, and to non-Euclidean destinations on a case-by-case basis. Orders over $75 ship free. Spare Tuesdays do not incur shipping, as they arrive independently.

Returns

You have 30 days from delivery to return any product in its original state. Moon Dust must be returned in a notarized envelope. Silence cannot be returned once opened. Fog is final sale.

Damaged items

If your item arrives damaged, email us at hello@wonders-oddities.example with a photograph and a short description of events. Please do not photograph Invisible Umbrellas; it is not useful.
item.excerpt:encoded: Where we ship, what we charge, and how to return a jar of silence.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Where we ship, what we charge, and how to return a jar of silence.

item.title: FAQ
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
Are your products real?

For most practical definitions of "real," yes. They are physical, shippable, and invoice-able. Whether they do what they say they do is a more complicated question that we address on a per-product basis.

Is the Mildly Haunted Candle dangerous?

The candle is dangerous only in the ways candles are dangerous. The haunting is polite. See Brenda's full write-up.

Can I buy Carl?

No.

Do Portable Holes expire?

Portable Holes do not expire. The items placed inside them are not retrievable.

Do you offer gift wrapping?

Yes, for $3 per item. Gift wrapping is not available on Fog in a Bottle.
item.excerpt:encoded: Answers to questions we've actually been asked.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Answers to questions we've actually been asked.

item.title: Contact
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
The shop's official correspondence address is a P.O. Box that exists primarily in the minds of our couriers. The digital address is hello@wonders-oddities.example, which we check on days that end in y.

For press inquiries: please direct them to Percival via the above. For supplier inquiries: please do not attempt to supply us with anything we have not asked for. Carl will be sad.

Hours

The physical shop is open Wednesday through Saturday, 10am to 6pm. The digital shop is always open. Spare Tuesdays, when available, are also open.
item.excerpt:encoded: How to reach the shop.

item.title: Privacy Policy
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
We collect standard shop information — names, shipping addresses, order history — for the express purpose of fulfilling orders and not for anything else. We do not sell, rent, or exchange customer data. We do not train machine-learning models on our customers, partly on principle and partly because our customers are too strange to generalize from.

Cookies

We use the minimum cookies required to keep you logged in and remember what's in your cart. We do not use analytics cookies or advertising cookies.

Your rights

You have the right to access, correct, or delete your data at any time. Email hello@wonders-oddities.example with the subject line "please don't remember me" and we will comply within 30 days. Your imaginary deeds remain, imaginatively, in your possession.
item.excerpt:encoded: What we collect, why, and our legal posture thereon.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: What we collect, why, and our legal posture thereon.

item.title: Welcome to Wonders & Oddities
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
A year ago, to the day, this shop did not exist. Which is to say: it existed in the way most things exist before they are built, which is to say vaguely, in the kitchen, around 2am, while the kettle reboiled.

Today it exists in the way shops exist: with inventory, invoices, a slightly temperamental card reader, and a small population of regulars who bring us pastries.

What we stock, in brief

We stock small, difficult objects. Bottled Monday Morning. Left Socks of Destiny. A Portable Hole in three sizes. Moon Dust of unverified origin. Forbidden Honey. Mildly Haunted Candles. A sampler of existential dread repellent, which moves better than you'd expect in the current climate.

We do not stock anything that is actually dangerous, with the cautious exception of the Pocket-Sized Thunder, which is only dangerous to ceilings and certain breeds of dog.

What we do not stock

  • Anything that is actually useful.
  • Anything a reasonable person would want to eat in quantity.
  • Anything you could explain at a dinner party without qualification.

Thank you for being here. We will continue to be here, as long as the Tuesdays hold.
item.excerpt:encoded: A brief note from the proprietor on the shop's first anniversary, what we stock, and why none of it is returnable once Tuesday has ended.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Welcome to Wonders & Oddities — Wonders & Oddities
A brief note from the proprietor on the shop's first anniversary, what we stock, and why none of it is returnable once Tuesday has ended.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Bought a Moon Dust tin last week. Carl's certificate is now framed above my desk.
The shop is the best thing to happen to this block since the coffee shop closed, and it is better than the coffee shop was.
Kind of you to say. We have been considering also selling coffee.
Do you ship to Japan? (I'm happy to wait.)

item.title: Year One, Abridged
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
We shipped 2,114 parcels. We received 1 return. We mis-shipped once, to a person who ordered Silence and received Fog; they wrote to say the fog had been, on reflection, more useful.

Our best-selling product, somewhat predictably, was the Bottled Monday Morning. Our worst-selling product, somewhat predictably, was the Certificate of Imaginary Ownership, which asks too much of its buyer.

Numbers you will find reassuring

  • Total products shipped: 2,114
  • Countries reached: 41
  • Countries we're not supposed to ship to: 2 (we did not)
  • Tuesdays issued: 187
  • Complaints about Tuesdays: 0

Numbers you will find unsettling

  • Objects dropped into Portable Holes, per customer estimate: 614
  • Objects recovered from Portable Holes: 0

Onward to year two. The tea is steeping.
item.excerpt:encoded: Twelve months of shopkeeping, summarized in one post, with footnotes.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Twelve months of shopkeeping, summarized in one post, with footnotes.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: The Fog-for-Silence switcheroo is my favorite customer service story I've ever read.
I've filed a note to double-check the Silence jars before we box them. We do not need a repeat.

item.title: Caring for Your Portable Hole
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
Every few weeks, a customer writes in to say they have lost something into their Portable Hole and would like it back. We must reply, kindly, that this is not how Portable Holes work. You cannot reach into a Portable Hole. You can only put things into it.

Rules of the hole

  1. Never place the Hole on a ceiling. Items placed in the Hole will still travel downward relative to the Hole's opening, which is now pointed at your head.
  2. Never place the Hole on yourself. We should not have to say this. We have said this.
  3. Do not attempt to fold the Hole. Folding a Hole creates a secondary Hole that goes sideways, which is worse.
  4. Store flat when not in use. Preferably in a drawer. Label the drawer.

What to put in the Hole

  • Embarrassing receipts
  • Old to-do lists
  • A lock of your own hair that you should have thrown out years ago
  • Feelings that cannot be processed through other channels

What not to put in the Hole

  • Your car keys
  • Your cat
  • A loved one, even in jest
  • Any part of yourself

Follow these rules and your Portable Hole will provide years of service. Don't, and it will provide the same service, but with consequences.
item.excerpt:encoded: A practical guide to owning a Portable Hole without losing your keys, your cat, or your sense of proportion.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Caring for Your Portable Hole — Wonders & Oddities
A practical guide to owning a Portable Hole without losing your keys, your cat, or your sense of proportion.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: WISH I had read this before step 4 of the instructions that came in the box, which I made up.
We are adding this post to the print insert in v2.
Where do the things go, though?
We do not know. We have stopped asking.

item.title: Caring for Your Portable Hole featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: How to Apply Existential Dread Repellent
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
Existential Dread Repellent is, to our surprise, our second best-selling product. We attribute this to the news cycle, the age demographics of our mailing list, and the simple fact that it smells like lavender and stops you thinking about mortality for approximately an afternoon.

When to apply

Apply in the morning, directly after coffee but before reading the news. Reapply at 4pm, if necessary. Do not apply before bed; the repellent is formulated to be energizing and can disrupt sleep.

Where to apply

  • Inner wrists (recommended)
  • Temples (for acute episodes)
  • Behind the ears (formal occasions)
  • Never on the face. It stings.

What it will not fix

The dread itself. The repellent repels, it does not dissolve. The underlying condition remains. See a therapist, call your mother, take a walk. We are a shop, not a treatment plan.
item.excerpt:encoded: Roll-on or spray? Wrists or temples? A short guide to getting the most out of our second-most-popular product.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: How to Apply Existential Dread Repellent — Wonders & Oddities
Roll-on or spray? Wrists or temples? A short guide to getting the most out of our second-most-popular product.
how to apply existential dread repellent

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Four hours in the hiking trails with this on, genuinely life-changing. I know you said it doesn't fix the dread. But it helped me not think about it for long enough to enjoy the trees.

item.title: How to Apply Existential Dread Repellent featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: A Recipe Roundup: What to Make with Chaos Seasoning
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
Chaos Seasoning is the only product in the shop we can't predict. The ingredients are known (salt, pepper, cumin, and one we don't name), but the proportions vary by shake, by jar, and apparently by the weather. This makes it a nightmare for recipes that need precision and a delight for recipes that don't.

1. Roasted potatoes

Cut potatoes into chunks, toss with olive oil, apply Chaos generously. Roast at 425°F for 40 minutes. Each wedge will taste slightly different, which is how potatoes ought to be served.

2. Popcorn

Make popcorn, butter it, shake Chaos over it, toss. Serve at a movie night where no one knows what to watch. The seasoning will provide the only unifying force.

3. Avocado toast (the world's smuggest use)

We apologize for putting "avocado toast" in print. But a single shake of Chaos will elevate yours. We hear from customers that a lime is also involved, sometimes.

4. Soft scrambled eggs

Eggs, butter, low heat, patience. A pinch of Chaos at the end. Do not stir after adding.

5. Grilled cheese

A shake of Chaos inside the sandwich, before the top slice goes on. The melt will fix everything.
item.excerpt:encoded: Chaos Seasoning tastes different every time you use it. Here are five recipes that benefit from that quality.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: A Recipe Roundup: What to Make with Chaos Seasoning — Wonders & Oddities
Chaos Seasoning tastes different every time you use it. Here are five recipes that benefit from that quality.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Did #1 with sweet potatoes instead. Best thing I have eaten in weeks.
The mystery ingredient... is it paprika?
We couldn't possibly say.

item.title: A Recipe Roundup: What to Make with Chaos Seasoning featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: On the Philosophy of Bottled Monday Morning
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
A customer wrote last week to ask whether Bottled Monday Morning was, in fact, still a Monday once it had been bottled. The question is better than it looks.

A Monday, stripped of its context, is just 24 hours. It is the context that makes it a Monday: the weight of the week ahead, the inertia of the weekend behind, the quiet insistence that one must now start again. Remove any of those, and you do not have a Monday. You have a Tuesday that is pretending.

Our Bottled Monday Morning retains its context. The liquid inside each flask is not a sample of caffeinated water; it is a sample of a specific morning, carefully preserved. Open the flask and the Monday re-expresses itself, with all the gloom intact. This is the product's entire promise.

Why would you want that? Why would anyone? The answer varies. Some customers use it for productivity: if the feeling of Monday is what makes Monday productive, why not summon it on a Friday afternoon, when the week's work is not yet done? Others use it for catharsis: the feeling of Monday, out of its natural context, is also funny. And a few customers — we have heard from these customers — use it as a warning to themselves. They keep an unopened flask on their desk as a reminder that the week is coming.

A Monday, bottled, is still a Monday. But it is a Monday you have asked for. That is the crucial difference.
item.excerpt:encoded: If you can bottle a Monday, is it still a Monday? A long-ish essay on time, coffee, and capture.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: On the Philosophy of Bottled Monday Morning — Wonders & Oddities
If you can bottle a Monday, is it still a Monday? A long-ish essay on time, coffee, and capture.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I'm going to think about this for approximately nine Mondays.
Keeping one on my desk as suggested. The effect is real.

item.title: On the Philosophy of Bottled Monday Morning featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: Memoirs of a Left Sock
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
The Left Sock of Destiny, we are told, is aware. We do not fully endorse this claim. We sell the sock as a single item, wool-blend, sized by foot and colored by availability. It is not, by our accounts, a sentient object. And yet.

Last month, a customer returned their Left Sock along with a small spiral-bound notebook. The notebook, they said, had appeared in their laundry basket, folded inside the sock. They had not written in it. They did not know who had. They wanted us to have it.

What follows is one entry from the notebook, reproduced verbatim, with the customer's permission.

Day 41. The foot is warm today. She walked us to the library and back. The right sock, which came from a different factory, does not speak. I do not know if it cannot or if it chooses not to. I have decided to assume the latter. It is more generous.

Day 42. I have been in the dryer. It was louder than I had been led to expect.

Draw your own conclusions. We make no claims.
item.excerpt:encoded: From the private writings of a Left Sock of Destiny, recovered from a thrift store in Pittsburgh.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Memoirs of a Left Sock — Wonders & Oddities
From the private writings of a Left Sock of Destiny, recovered from a thrift store in Pittsburgh.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Do NOT put me through this again. I read this on the bus and laughed loudly enough to startle a child.
Sorry, Rollo. Not sorry.

item.title: Memoirs of a Left Sock featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: The Art of Artisanal Silence
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
The silence jar is our slowest product to make. Not because silence is rare — silence is, if anything, too plentiful — but because useful silence is rare. The kind of silence you want to pour into a jar is the kind that has been earned by long contemplation, good coffee, and the nearby hum of a forest.

We source ours from a monastery that does not wish to be named. The brothers and sisters there have been practicing silence for sixty-two years. They are very good at it. Every Tuesday morning, between 7am and 9am, we collect approximately two liters' worth of silence in specialized glass, seal it on-site, and ship it home in padded crates. We do not collect on Mondays — the silence is always a bit brittle on Mondays — nor on weekends, when the silence is compromised by visitors.

Each jar contains approximately 8 ounces. Once opened, the silence disperses. You have perhaps two minutes. Use them well.
item.excerpt:encoded: What it takes to harvest silence at a monastery, and why we only do it on weekdays.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Art of Artisanal Silence — Wonders & Oddities
What it takes to harvest silence at a monastery, and why we only do it on weekdays.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Brenda, is there a waitlist for the next batch?
Yes, email us. We cap the list at 200.

item.title: The Art of Artisanal Silence featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: An Interview with Carl, Our Lunar Goods Supplier
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
I met Carl in the storeroom, where he was rearranging the Moon Dust tins by density. He agreed to be interviewed on the condition that we not publish his last name, which is a condition we respected, because Carl has never told us his last name.

Carl, where does the Moon Dust come from?

Carl: The moon, Percival.

Specifically.

Carl: A specific part of the moon, Percival.

How did you get there?

Carl: I didn't say I went there. I said it comes from there.

So you receive shipments from the moon.

Carl: I receive shipments.

Carl, is any of this Moon Dust actually from the moon?

Carl: Percival, the framing of the question presupposes a binary I do not endorse. Things can be from the moon in different ways.

I changed the subject after that. Carl and I had a cup of tea, which he supplied. The tea was very good. I did not ask where it was from.
item.excerpt:encoded: Carl supplies our Moon Dust. We finally asked Carl where it comes from.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: An Interview with Carl, Our Lunar Goods Supplier — Wonders & Oddities
Carl supplies our Moon Dust. We finally asked Carl where it comes from.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I love Carl. Carl is never going to answer the question.
I answered the question. Percival asked several questions. Not all of them were good faith.
They were all good faith, Carl. We are just poor at corroborating.

item.title: An Interview with Carl, Our Lunar Goods Supplier featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: Customer Spotlight: What People Do with Invisible Umbrellas
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
The Invisible Umbrella is, from a use-case perspective, a difficult product. It keeps you dry. It does not keep the onlooker informed. We asked customers how they were using theirs.

The practical

Anna D. reports using her umbrella during outdoor photography sessions, because photographers traditionally do not love umbrellas in the frame. "Now the rain bounces off nothing, which looks appropriately magical."

The theatrical

Boris K. performs at children's parties. "I walk in from the rain completely dry. The children are upset. Their parents are thrilled."

The regretful

M. Thornton misplaces their umbrella approximately twice a week. This was a foreseeable complication. We should have warned them. We will add a warning to the product page.

To submit your own story, email hello@wonders-oddities.example. We read every submission. We reply to most.
item.excerpt:encoded: We asked customers what they've been doing with our most disorienting product. The answers ranged from the practical to the absurd.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Customer Spotlight: What People Do with Invisible Umbrellas — Wonders & Oddities
We asked customers what they've been doing with our most disorienting product. The answers ranged from the practical to the absurd.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: I have misplaced mine a third time since this post was published. Please do not add a warning. I want others to learn as I did.

item.title: Customer Spotlight: What People Do with Invisible Umbrellas featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: Hidden Gems: 5 Products You've Overlooked
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
A gentle reminder: the shop has thirty products, not just the five that dominate our sales reports. Here are five we'd love you to spend time with.

1. Spare Key to Nowhere

A brass key that fits no known lock. The most relaxing object we sell. People keep them on their keychains. There is something comforting about a key that cannot possibly open a door.

2. Fog in a Bottle (Coastal)

Two cubic meters of genuine coastal fog. Decorative. Not edible. Pair with a Mildly Haunted Candle for full effect.

3. Handcrafted Echo

A custom echo, tuned to the canyon of your choice. Wind it up, release it. The echo will find the nearest hard surface and rebound with appropriate delay. Incredible in bathrooms.

4. Memory Foam Memory

Not a pillow. Not a mattress. A standalone memory, impressed into memory foam. Three choices: Beach Day, Warm Kitchen, Rainy Afternoon. Place on nightstand.

5. Gently Used Luck

Pre-owned luck, sold by the ounce. Condition varies. We sell it cheap because we believe, and we want you to believe, that the good luck is not yet fully used up.
item.excerpt:encoded: Our best-sellers get the love. These five deserve more.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Hidden Gems: 5 Products You've Overlooked — Wonders & Oddities
Our best-sellers get the love. These five deserve more.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Ordering the Spare Key immediately.

item.title: Hidden Gems: 5 Products You've Overlooked featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: Seasonal Picks: Fog Season Is Upon Us
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
The damp months are here. The shop's product mix is responding accordingly. A small list, lightly curated.

  • Fog in a Bottle — of course
  • Mildly Haunted Candle — for atmosphere
  • Invisible Umbrella — dry without visual noise
  • Bottled Monday Morning — drink during, not before

Pair any two. Light a candle. Put on a kettle. The season will pass, slowly.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short seasonal recommendation post for the damp months.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Seasonal Picks: Fog Season Is Upon Us — Wonders & Oddities
A short seasonal recommendation post for the damp months.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Bought three. Thank you.

item.title: Seasonal Picks: Fog Season Is Upon Us featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: A Field Guide to Spare Tuesdays
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
A Spare Tuesday, once received, expires at midnight on the day it's been inserted. You cannot bank it, trade it, or subdivide it. The day exists. You may do what you like with it.

What to try

  • Sleep through it
  • Finish one thing you've been putting off
  • Call someone you've been meaning to
  • Read an entire book

What not to try

  • Two Tuesdays in a row (does not work)
  • Work (defeats the point)
  • Explaining it to your employer (they will not understand)
item.excerpt:encoded: How to use a Spare Tuesday when you get one. Includes what not to try.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: A Field Guide to Spare Tuesdays — Wonders & Oddities
How to use a Spare Tuesday when you get one. Includes what not to try.

item.title: A Field Guide to Spare Tuesdays featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: Behind the Scenes: How We Bottle Mondays
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
Several customers have asked how, exactly, we bottle the Mondays. We have always been evasive in our answer. We remain evasive today, but we'll tell you more than we have before.

The bottling happens between 6:47am and 7:15am, local time, every Monday except national holidays and the one Monday in 2025 when we overslept. The location is, or rather was, a commuter rail platform in a northern-Ohio town that we will not name, because our contract with the town stipulates that we will not name it.

The Monday is captured by specialized funnels that Brenda designed. The flasks are food-safe borosilicate with hand-stamped caps. Each flask holds approximately 4 ounces. We produce between 200 and 300 per Monday, depending on the reluctance of the commuters present.

And that is what we can tell you about the Mondays.
item.excerpt:encoded: We can't tell you everything. But we can tell you some of it.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Behind the Scenes: How We Bottle Mondays — Wonders & Oddities
We can't tell you everything. But we can tell you some of it.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Brenda, DM me, I have a funnel question.
Sunil, email. No funnel DMs.

item.title: Behind the Scenes: How We Bottle Mondays featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: The Mildly Haunted Candle FAQ
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
Is the haunting real?

We do not use the word "real" in this context. The haunting is, however, reliable. Customers report it on about 73% of candles, which is higher than chance.

Does the entity move into my home?

No. The entity remains at the candle. When the candle is extinguished, so is the haunting. When the candle is fully consumed, the entity returns, presumably, to wherever it came from.

Is it safe for pets?

The candle is as safe for pets as any candle. The entity has never harmed an animal, to our knowledge, although one customer reported that their cat was unusually friendly near the candle. The customer found this uplifting.

Can I request a specific entity?

No.
item.excerpt:encoded: Everything you've asked us about the candle, in one place.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Mildly Haunted Candle FAQ — Wonders & Oddities
Everything you've asked us about the candle, in one place.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: My cat loves this candle. I am either concerned or very not concerned, I can't tell.

item.title: The Mildly Haunted Candle FAQ featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: What Does "Forbidden" Mean in "Forbidden Honey"?
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
The Forbidden Honey is called "forbidden" for the following reasons, in rough order of importance:

  1. It is sourced from hives that exist in a legal gray area between two counties.
  2. The supplier has asked us not to name the counties.
  3. Neither county has asked us to stop selling the honey.
  4. It tastes forbidden. We cannot describe the flavor. Customers know.

None of this should be construed as a legal opinion, an endorsement of illegal apiculture, or a recommendation that you explore the gray-area-county-boundary honey market on your own. Buy it from us. We have done the paperwork, such as there is.
item.excerpt:encoded: A short note on product names, legal jurisdictions, and the taste of things.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: What Does "Forbidden" Mean in "Forbidden Honey"? — Wonders & Oddities
A short note on product names, legal jurisdictions, and the taste of things.
forbidden honey what does forbidden mean

item.title: What Does "Forbidden" Mean in "Forbidden Honey"? featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: Pocket-Sized Thunder Safety: A Cautionary Tale
item.dc:creator: brenda-ash
item.content:encoded:
We want to be delicate here. The customer in question is a regular, a thoughtful person, a professional, and a parent. They ordered three Pocket-Sized Thunders last quarter. They are not to blame.

What they did, we are told, was deploy one Pocket-Sized Thunder indoors, in their living room, at a cocktail party, as a demonstration to friends. The demonstration was successful. The drywall is also fine. The cat is, as of this writing, still under a bed.

The takeaway

The Pocket-Sized Thunder produces 85-102 decibels of sound, concentrated in a very short burst. This is comparable to a leaf blower at close range. Leaf blowers, too, are not to be deployed indoors, regardless of the quality of the demonstration. Please.

The product page has been updated. The cat, we are told, will come out eventually.
item.excerpt:encoded: A customer deployed Pocket Thunder indoors. This is the story of what happened next.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Pocket-Sized Thunder Safety: A Cautionary Tale — Wonders & Oddities
A customer deployed Pocket Thunder indoors. This is the story of what happened next.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: The cat is out. Thanks for checking.
Relieved.

item.title: Pocket-Sized Thunder Safety: A Cautionary Tale featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: A Beginner's Guide to Imaginary Ownership
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
Our Certificate of Imaginary Ownership is not a popular product. We understand why. It asks something of the owner that other products do not: to sustain a belief about a thing that does not exist.

What you own

A certificate. An idea. The right to describe the property to others. The right to visit it, mentally, whenever you please.

What you do not own

The property itself. Any rights to the property in this universe. Any income or utility from the property. Tax liabilities, jurisdictional authority, or resale value.

Is it worth it?

It is worth exactly what you are willing to believe it is worth. This is, we submit, not that different from most other forms of ownership, if you sit with the question long enough.
item.excerpt:encoded: What you actually own when you own something imaginary. A short guide.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: A Beginner's Guide to Imaginary Ownership — Wonders & Oddities
What you actually own when you own something imaginary. A short guide.

item.title: A Beginner's Guide to Imaginary Ownership featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath

item.title: Tangible Wi-Fi: A 30-Day Review
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
item.content:encoded:
Tangible Wi-Fi is the strangest object the shop has ever stocked, which is a category that includes a Portable Hole and a sock that arguably writes journal entries. We took one home for a month to see what living with the internet, as a physical object, was actually like.

Day 1: First impressions

The unit is approximately the size and weight of a tennis ball. It hums faintly. It is warm. It is, against expectations, not unpleasant to hold. The Wi-Fi continues to function in the rest of the apartment, which surprised us.

Day 7: Routines

By the end of the first week, the unit had taken up residence on the kitchen counter, between the kettle and the fruit bowl. The cat, who has strong opinions about new objects, was indifferent. The household considered this an endorsement.

Day 14: Children

The visiting nephew (age 8) attempted to play catch with the Wi-Fi. The Wi-Fi, mercifully, did not break. The nephew was deeply impressed. Connection speeds did not noticeably degrade. We have nonetheless added "no catch" to the household rules.

Day 21: A test

We took the unit to the back garden, well outside the router's normal range. Streaming continued. Web pages loaded. The unit grew slightly warmer. We returned indoors, somewhat unnerved.

Day 30: Verdict

The product does what it claims. The product is, mildly, the most disorienting useful object we have ever owned. The household will continue to use it. The household will not, however, recommend it to people who have a low tolerance for low-grade ontological strangeness in their kitchen.

Five stars, if the rating system supports half-stars. Four-and-a-half if not.
item.excerpt:encoded: We took our most science-fictional product home for a month. Here's what 30 days of holding the internet looked like.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Tangible Wi-Fi: A 30-Day Review — Wonders & Oddities
We took our most science-fictional product home for a month. Here's what 30 days of holding the internet looked like.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: The phrase "low-grade ontological strangeness" is going on my mug.
Mine has been on my desk for 6 weeks. The hum is comforting. I know how that sounds.

item.title: Carl: On the Moon, Generally
item.dc:creator: carl-lunar
item.content:encoded:
Percival gave me a blog. I said thank you. I've been thinking about what to write for, at this point, four weeks. Here is what I have.

People ask me about the moon. They ask with a certain tone. The tone implies: Carl, prove it. I am never going to prove it. I don't need to. The moon is not provable by a man in a shop in Ohio. The moon is provable only by the moon, and the moon is quiet about the matter.

What I can tell you is that the dust is real. The dust shows up on my dock every second Tuesday. Percival has watched it arrive. Brenda has sniffed it and not sneezed, which she says is diagnostic. The dust is real.

Where the dust comes from, I maintain, is a separate question. We sell you dust. We do not sell you certainty. If we sold certainty, the price would be different.
item.excerpt:encoded: Carl's first post. It is about the moon. It is not, strictly, about the moon.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Carl: On the Moon, Generally — Wonders & Oddities
Carl's first post. It is about the moon. It is not, strictly, about the moon.

wp:comment.wp:comment_content: Carl, welcome to the blog.
I take some issue with the framing around "provability," but we will discuss this over tea.

item.title: Carl: On the Moon, Generally featured
item.dc:creator: percival-aftermath
